coredata.dc:title: A highly proton conductive membrane based on hydrolyzed NbCl5 and phytic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ming-Yu
Wang, Yi-Di
Cheng, Dongming
Liu, Bai-Ling
Wang, Yong-Hui
Zang, Hong-Ying
coredata.dc:description: It is vital to choose economical and environmentally friendly proton conductive materials to improve the performance of proton exchange membranes, which occupies a unique position in proton exchange membrane fuel cells. This paper reports a new proton conductive nanocomposite, that was named NbO2(OH)-PA prepared from phytic acid (PA) and NbCl5. NbO2(OH)-PA showed an excellent proton conductivity of 135 mS cm−1 at 85 °C and 97% relative humidity. In addition, NbO2(OH)-PA was combined with sulfonated poly (ether ketone) (SPEEK) in different proportions to form proton conductive membranes, that are labeled SPEEK-x NbO2(OH)-PA. SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA exhibited the best proton conductivity of 0.17 S cm−1 at 80 °C in water. This work may provide new ideas for improving proton conductivity of membranes through simple methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NbCl5
Sol-gel method
Phytic acid
Proton conductive membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of membranes of SPEEK and SPEEK-NbO2(OH)-PA

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD pattern of NbO2(OH)-PA. (b) The SEM micrograph of NbO2(OH)-PA. (c) The FI-IR spectra of NbO2(OH)-PA. (d) The TGA curve of NbO2(OH)-PA.
(a) Nb 3d XPS spectra. (b) P 2p XPS spectra. (c) Proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA at 25 °C under different humidity. (d) Water adsorption of NbO2(OH)-PA.

(a) Nyquist plots of NbO2(OH)-PA at 97% RH from 29 °C to 85 °C. (b) Nyquist plots of NbO2(OH)-PA at 97% RH from 29 °C to 65 °C. (c) Nyquist plots of NbO2(OH)-PA at 97% RH from 69 °C to 85 °C. (d) Arrhenius plots of the temperature-dependent proton conductivities of NbO2(OH)-PA.

(a) SEM cross-sectional micrographs of SPEEK. (b) SEM cross-sectional micrographs of SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA.(c) Arrhenius plots of the SPEEK-x NbO2(OH)-PA membrane from 30 °C to 80 °C. (d) Nyquist plots of SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA in water from 30 °C to 80 °C.

head.ce:title: A highly proton conductive membrane based on hydrolyzed NbCl5 and phytic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Polyoxometalate Science of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Polyoxometalate Science of Ministry of Education
Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Polyoxometalate Science of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is vital to choose economical and environmentally friendly proton conductive materials to improve the performance of proton exchange membranes, which occupies a unique position in proton exchange membrane fuel cells. This paper reports a new proton conductive nanocomposite, that was named NbO2(OH)-PA prepared from phytic acid (PA) and NbCl5. NbO2(OH)-PA showed an excellent proton conductivity of 135 mS cm−1 at 85 °C and 97% relative humidity. In addition, NbO2(OH)-PA was combined with sulfonated poly (ether ketone) (SPEEK) in different proportions to form proton conductive membranes, that are labeled SPEEK-x NbO2(OH)-PA. SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA exhibited the best proton conductivity of 0.17 S cm−1 at 80 °C in water. This work may provide new ideas for improving proton conductivity of membranes through simple methods.

Economic and environmentally friendly phytic acid can be used in highly proton conductive materials and can be applied to proton exchange membranes.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the growing social concern about the issues of environment, energy consumption and sustainable development, fuel cells are important sources of energy conversion devices because they use clean energy as fuel and rarely generate pollutants during operation [1, 2]. Proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) are expected to become the main development trend of energy-saving and environmentally-friendly mobile power equipment due to their high specific power, simple structure, outstanding energy conversion efficiency [3–6]. Proton exchange membranes, as a core component, determine whether PEMFCs can perform well in operation. At present, Nafion membranes are the most widely used in commerce due to its high proton conducivity and physicothermal characteristics [7–9], but their shortcomings such as high cost, complex manufacturing processes and the proton conductivity will decrease after dehydration at high temperature have led researchers to continuously explore alternatives [10–12]. Among them, sulfonated poly (ether ketone) (SPEEK) is one of the most potential candidates due to its low-cost, excellent thermal stability, mechanical properties, and high proton conductivity [13–17]. However, SPEEK still has certain limitations in the application of fuel cells. For example, the proton conductivity and swelling rate depend on the degree of sulfonation, which is not easy to control [17,18]. Thus more and more attention is attracted by introducing efficient proton conductive materials into the SPEEK skeleton to improve its performance [19]. For example, Zhou et al. developed the SPEEK/HPW@MSNs with a high proton conductivity, which was 26.78% higher than that of the pristine SPEEK membrane [20]. In addition, several new materials with superior proton conductivity have been explored, such as phosphoric acid [21] and sulfonic acid derivatives [22], polyoxometalates [23], metal-organic frameworks [24,25], covalent-organic frameworks [26] and carbon allotropes [27]. But the above materials require complex synthetic methods under relatively harsh conditions and some starting materials are very expensive. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to choose a low-cost and environmentally-friendly material to design excellent proton conductive materials [28]. At the same time, the selection of appropriate synthesis methods and routes has also attracted the focus of researchers [29]. The sol-gel method has been developed for its mild and flexible synthesis route, energy efficiency and high production rate. More importantly, this method can ensure that the introduced molecules can be evenly distributed in the host skeleton and maintain long-term stability. In addition, the sol-gel method also maked it easier for the material to form particles of uniform size [30–33].

Phytic acid (PA), an organic phosphate compound extracted from plant seeds, is widely used in chemical, food, biosensor, anticorrosion, cation exchange resin, pharmaceutical and other scientific research fields due to its green, low-cost, abundant, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility [34–37]. Twelve acidic protons contained on six phosphate groups of PA make it ocuupy an important position in the design of proton-conducting materials due to their excellent proton conductivity, low water dependence, excellent chemical and thermal stability, and high proton mobility [38,39]. The content of phosphate groups make PA easy to interact with metal ions and positively charged molecules, and can also act as a source of groups that form hydrogen bond networks. Therefore, PA is being gradually recorded and reported in the field of proton conduction due to its unique characteristics. Jiang et al. designed PA@MIL101 by incorporating phytic acid molecules into MIL101 by vacuum assisted impregnation method,and PA@MIL101 was further filled into Nafion to form a proton exchange membrane with a proton conductivity of 0.0608 S cm−1 [40]. Rahul Banerjee's project improved the anhydrous proton conductivity of raw materials TpPa–SO3H–Py to 5 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 120 °C by introducing phytic acid molecules into the framework [41]. Therefore, it is very promising to select phytic acid to design efficient proton conductive materials.

Amorphous nanomaterials based on transition metal oxyhydroxide (MOx (OH)) have attracted widespread attention due to their unique properties [42,43], which can effectively construct a hydrogen bonding network due to its abundant hydroxyl groups and free water molecules [44,45], making MOx (OH) start to attract attention in the field of proton conduction [46]. In addition, the analogous nanoparticles have the advantages of fluorescence, non-toxicity, good biocompatibility and photocatalytic activity, but their proton conduction properties have been rarely reported [47–51]. As a strong Lewis acid. Niobium pentachloride was easily hydrolyzed to form niobium oxyhydroxide (NbO2(OH)) due to the large electronegativity difference between Nb and Cl. Meamwhile, NbO2(OH) also can be used as a precursor of a series of nanomaterials, it has been studied due to its good thermal stability and water resistance in common transition metal materials [52–54]. NbO2(OH) can be obtained under acidic conditions. The presence of incompletely coordinated Nb5+ on the surface makes hydrolyzd NbCl5 exhibit strong Lewis acidity and the hydroxyl group attached to the niobium atom can be used as a carrier for proton transfer. Futhermore, phosphate groups in PA and hydroxyl groups in NbO2(OH) can form hydrogen bond network to provide a large number of jumping sites for proton conduction, enabling the material to achieve high proton conductivity. Our previous research has shown that PW12-doped niobium oxyhydroxide can exhibit a proton conductivity of 6.52 × 10−3 S cm−1 under high temperature and high humidity, which indicates the feasibility of niobium oxyhydroxide as a proton conductor [55].

In this paper, we have prepared a highly efficient proton conductive material NbO2(OH)-PA using phytic acid and NbCl5 with a simple sol-gel method, which could achieve a high conductivity value of 135 mS cm−1 at 85 °C with excellent stability. It is a new strategy for designing low-cost and efficient proton conductive materials. Meanwhile, Introducing NbO2(OH)-PA into the SPEEK skeleton to form a proton exchange membrane, which can effectively ameliorate the proton conductivity and methanol permeability of the original SPEEK.

NbO2(OH)-PA was obtained via the sol-gel method. The synthetic procedure was as follows: NbCl5 (1.35 g, 5 mmol) was fully dissolved in 6 M HCl (20 mL) and phytic acid was added into the mixed solution in proportion. The mixed solution was stirred for 2 h to fully hydrolyze to form gels, then transferred to a dialysis tube and washed with an amount of deionized water until the pH of filtrate turned to 4. During the process, niobium oxyhydroxide formed by hydrolysis of NbCl5 interacted with phytic acid to form a kind of white product, named as NbO2(OH)-PA. The products were finally obtained by freeze-drying; (NbCl5:PA molar ratio is 20:1, 30:1, 40:1, 50:1, 60:1, 70:1, respectively.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of membranes of SPEEK and SPEEK-NbO2(OH)-PA
ce:section.ce:para: SPEEK was synthesized according to a previous literature [56]. SPEEK (0.5 g) and NbO2(OH)-PA were added to 5 mL of DMAC in proportion, and the mixed solution was sonicated for 4 h and stirred continuously for 12 h. The resulting solution was casted onto a clean glass plate, dried in air for 24 h, then removing the solvent at 80 °C for 12 h by vacuum drying. The membrane obtained was immersed in 1 M H2SO4 and water at 25 °C for 24 h, respectively. The proton-conducting membrane was named by SPEEK-x NbO2(OH)-PA, x referred to the weight percentage of NbO2(OH)-PA relative to SPEEK.

The IR spectrum of NbO2(OH)-PA was showed in Fig. 1 c. The absorption vibration peaks of Nb–O–Nb bond and H–O–H bond corresponded to 492 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1 in NbO2(OH), respectively. The peak at 986 cm−1 and a series of peaks in the range of 850 to 650 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic peaks of PO (ν
s ) and P–O–Nb, respectively. In addition, The energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) spectra of NbO2(OH)-PA was given in Fig. S1, The peaks corresponding to Nb, P and O elements were observed clearly. Those proved that PA had been successfully incorporated into the NbO2(OH).

Fig. 1a showed that the XRD pattern of NbO2(OH)-PA has only a broad peak, indicating NbO2(OH)-PA was an amorphous state. Meanwhile, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs was given in Fig. 1b. In order to calculate the size of the material, the measurement of the particle size of NbO2(OH)-PA and the columnar distribution diagram are shown in Fig. S1, which indicatied NbO2(OH)-PA was nanoparticles with the size of ca. 160 nm. As shown in Fig. S3, the N2 adsorption-desorption test of the NbO2(OH)-PA illustrated similar characters to type II adsorption-desorption curve, which did not show obvious hysteresis loops. The calculation showed that the specific surface area of the NbO2(OH)-PA was 31.68 m2 g−1. Such extremely low specific surface further illustrated that NbO2(OH)-PA is a kind of non-porous material.

Fig. 1d showed the TGA curve of NbO2(OH)-PA. Two major weight-loss stages could be observed with the increasing temperature. The first stage weight loss occurred in the range of 20–170 °C,which could attributed to the removal of free and bound water molecules in NbO2(OH)-PA. The other stage weight loss between 470–520 °C was due to the decomposition of nioboum oxyhydroxide. The water retention and stability of NbO2(OH)-PA offered the possibility to achieve the high proton conductivity.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to demonstrate elemental valence and the existence of composite materials. The high-resolution XPS spectra of NbO2(OH)-PA were shown in Fig. 2 a and b. The peaks of Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2 corresponded to 207.8 eV and 210.5 eV, respectively, which were consistent with the characteristic peaks of Nb5+. In addition, there was only one peak of 133.8 eV in the spectrum of P 2p, which was the characteristic peak of P5+ in PA. The analysis results showed that the elements in the composite existed in the form of Nb5+ and P5+.

To study the role of relative humidity in proton conduction of materials, the proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA was characterized at 25 °C and different relative humidity (RH) by alternating current impedance analysis. As shown in Fig. 2c, the proton conductivity of the material increased significantly as relative humidity raised, and reached a maximum of 3.740 mS cm−1 at 97% RH, suggesting that the high relative humidity was more favorable for proton transportation. In addition, the relationship between the water absorption and relative pressure of the material was determined by water vapor adsorption-desorption test, which was shown in Fig. 2d. The curve displayed that the water absorption of NbO2(OH)-PA increased as relative pressure raised, which meant water molecules occupied a significant position in proton transfer. Firstly, water molecules served as proton carriers to transfer protons. Secondly, water molecules would build ordered hydrogen bond networks with the phosphate and hydroxyl group in NbO2(OH)-PA. These features resulted in faster proton transport and exhibited higher proton conductivity.

Further test of the proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA was performed in the temperature range of 25 °C–85 °C under 97% RH. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b and Fig. 3c, a typical Nyquist plot showed a reduced diameter of semi-circular arc in high-frequency region, representing a significant increase in proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA as the temperature increased. Proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA reached 102 mS cm−1 and 135 mS cm−1 at 65 °C and 85 °C, respectively. The improved proton conductivity was attributed to the fact that high temperature could accelerate the movement of H3O+ as a proton carrier and activate the hydrogen bonding network. In order to prove the stability of NbO2(OH)-PA at high temperature, a proton conductive stability test of the material was performed at 85 °C and 97% RH (Fig. S1). The experimental results showed that the proton conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA with almost no attenuation within 24 h, which proved that NbO2(OH)-PA had excellent stability as a candidate for efficient proton conductive materials. Meanwhile, in order to eliminate the possibility of ion transfer, the conductivity of NbO2(OH)-PA was tested in D2O at 25 °C and 97% RH,as shown in Fig. S5, the material showed a lower conductivity of 2.42 mS cm−1, which indicated the conductivity in NbO2(OH)-PA was due to proton transfer.

To further explore the proton conduction mechanism of the compounds, the activation energy (Ea) of NbO2(OH)-PA was calculated according to the Arrhenius curve. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Ea value of NbO2(OH)-PA was calculated by linear regression to be 0.51 eV, which was close to the boundary of the two proton conduction mechanisms. The high activation energy of the NbO2(OH)-PA was presumed to a large amount of H3O+ combined by the free water molecules absorbed by NbO2(OH)-PA and the protons self-dissociated from the rich phosphate groups in phytic acid. The formed H3O+ acted as a proton carrier to self-diffuse in a fixed direction in space to perform proton transfer. At the same time, hydrogen bonding networks were also thought to play an important role in proton transfer. A continuous and ordered hydrogen bonding network was constructed by phosphate and hydroxyl group in NbO2(OH)-PA, which provided more proton hopping sites for improved proton-conducting behavior. This result indicated that the proton conduction mechanism of NbO2(OH)-PA might be a mixed mechanism that followed the vehicles mechanism and the Grotthuss mechanism. The effect of the amount of phytic acid doped on the materials had also been studied. As shown in Table S1, impedance tests on NbO2(OH)-PA materials with different proportions of phytic acid were conducted at 25 °C and 97% RH. The data showed that the proton conductivity of the material increased correspondingly as the amount of phytic acid increased and reached a maximum at 50:1. After that, proton conductivity of the materials decreased with the amount of phytic acid continued to increase. The appearance of this trend was presumed that when NbCl5 and phytic acid were hydrolyzed in situ, NbCl5 could not be fully hydrolyzed due to the high local concentration and viscosity of phytic acid.

Furthermore, the effects of the introduction of NbO2(OH)-PA on the performance of SPEEK had also been studied. The synthesis method of SPEEK-x NbO2(OH)-PA Different proportions of NbO2(OH)-PA were injected into SPEEK to form proton exchange membranes, that were labeled as SPEEK-0.6% NbO2(OH)-PA, SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA, SPEEK-1.2% NbO2(OH)-PA, and SPEEK-1.5% NbO2(OH)-PA. As shown in Fig. 4 c and d, the Arrhenius curves of the membranes showed SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA membrane had the best proton conductivity of 0.17 S cm−1 at 80 °C in water. This was comparable to the proton conductivity of a commercially available Nafion membrane. This proved that suitable NbO2(OH)-PA had a good effect on improving the proton conductivity of the SPEEK membrane. When the doping of NbO2(OH)-PA was 1.2% and 1.5%, respectively, the proton conductivity of the membranes was decreased, because the excessive doping would result in an uneven dispersion. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs in Fig. 4b clearly showed cross-sectional images of pristine SPEEK membrane and SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA. Compared to SEM of pristine SPEEK membranes in Fig. 4a, SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA showed that NbO2(OH)-PA particles were evenly distributed in the mesh structure of SPEEK. Therefore, we thought that NbO2(OH)-PA can act as a proton conductor in doped membranes, coupling with the sulfonic acid group (-SO3H) in SPEEK to form a continuous hydrogen bond network. Meanwhile, SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA displayed the Ion exchange capacity (IEC) of 2.167 mmol g−1, which was decreased compared to pristine SPEEK membrane (2.837% mmol g−1). It was attributed to the establishment of cross-linked network with part of free sulfonic acid groups in SPEEK and the hydroxyl and phosphate groups in NbO2(OH)-PA, thereby reducing the supply of protons. The mechanical properties of SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA and pure SPEEK were shown in Table S3, and tensile strength of SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA was about 44.5 Mpa, which was reduced compared to that of pristine SPEEK (65.7 Mpa). This might be attributed to the doping of NbO2(OH)-PA. In general, mechanical properties of membrane usually reduce, because the original structure membrane was destroyed by doping of inorganic particles.

In order to explore the durability of the composite, the proton conductivity of SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA was measured at 80 °C in water for 12 h. As shown in Fig. S6, the proton conductivity of membrane decreased with time, but still maintained at 0.1 S cm−1, which could provide feasibility for long-term work of composite membrane in fuel cells. At the same time, the membranes' oxidation resistance test (Table S3) showed that the membrane mass loss was 1.81% after soaking in Fenton reagent for 12 h which was better than pristine SPEEK membrane of 3.93%. It indicated that SPEEK-0.9% NbO2(OH)-PA had good stability under strict conditions. Methanol permeability is also consdered to be an important indicator for measuring the performance of proton exchange membranes. Table S2 showed that the methanol permeability of the composite membrane were 3.03 × 10−7 cm2 s−1 and 3.48 × 10−7 cm2 s−1 after 12 h and 24 h, respectively, which were less than pristine SPEEK under the same conditions, indicating that the doping of NbO2(OH)-PA could block the path of methanol permeation, thus improving the methanol penetration resistance of SPEEK effectively. In general, the proton conductivities of NbO2(OH)-PA and SPEEK-NbO2(OH)-PA were both higher than that of most proton-conducting materials based on niobium and PA (Table S4), which showed the great potential in the field of fuel cells.

In summary, a highly efficient proton conductive material NbO2(OH)-PA has been designed based on NbCl5 and phytic acid, which showed high proton conductivity of 135 mS cm−1 at 97% RH and 85 °C. Further, SPEEK acted as backbone of NbO2(OH)-PA to form hybrid proton conductive membranes SPEEK-NbO2(OH)-PA, showing a high proton conductivity of 0.17 S cm−1 at 80 °C, which was potential for application in fuel cells in the future. We believe that this work provides new ideas and platforms for using low-cost proton conductive materials to improve the performance of proton exchange membranes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471028, 21871042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2020.03.153.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review on parameter estimation techniques for Proton Exchange Membrane fuel cell modelling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Priya K, Sathishkumar K, Rajasekar N. A comprehensive review on parameter estimation techniques for Proton Exchange Membrane fuel cell modelling. Renew Sust Energ Rev 2018;93:121-144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical challenges in the system development of direct alcohol fuel cells as portable power supplies: an overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fadzillah DM, Kamarudin SK, Zainoodin MA, Masdar MS. Critical challenges in the system development of direct alcohol fuel cells as portable power supplies: An overview. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2019;44:3031-3054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on modelling of high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells (HT-PEMFCs)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abdul Rasheed RK, Liao K, Zhang C, Chan SH. A review on modelling of high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells (HT-PEMFCs). Int J Hydrogen Energy 2017;42:3142-3165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation aspects of water formation and transport in proton exchange membrane fuel cell: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ous T, Arcoumanis C. Degradation aspects of water formation and transport in proton exchange membrane fuel cell: a review. J Power Sources 2013;240:558-582.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proton exchange membranes prepared via atom transfer radical polymerization for proton exchange membrane fuel cell: recent advances and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao Y, Li X, Wang SB, Li WW, Wang XK, Chen ST, Chen J, Xie XF. Proton exchange membranes prepared via atom transfer radical polymerization for proton exchange membrane fuel cell: recent advances and perspectives. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2017;42:30013-30028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dramatic improvement in water retention and proton conductivity in electrically aligned functionalized CNT/SPEEK nanohybrid PEM

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Swati G, Vaibhav K. Dramatic improvement in water retention and proton conductivity in electrically aligned functionalized CNT/SPEEK nanohybrid PEM. Acs Appl Mater Interfaces 2015;7:264-272.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: GO-nafion composite membrane development for enabling intermediate temperature operation of polymer electrolyte fuel cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ibrahim A, Hossain O, Chaggar J, Steinberger-Wilckens R, El-Kharouf A. GO-nafion composite membrane development for enabling intermediate temperature operation of polymer electrolyte fuel cell. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2020;45:5526-5534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent approaches to improve Nafion performance for fuel cell applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Karimi MB, Mohammadi M, Hooshyari K. Recent approaches to improve Nafion performance for fuel cell applications: A review. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2019;44:28919-28938.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on nafion-based nanocomposite membranes for fuel cell applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cele N, Ray SS. Recent Progress on Nafion-Based Nanocomposite Membranes for Fuel Cell Applications. Macromol Mater Eng 2009;294:719-738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and properties of functional hybrid organic-inorganic membranes for fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Laberty-Robert C, Valle K, Pereira F, Sanchez C. Design and properties of functional hybrid organic-inorganic membranes for fuel cells. Chem Soc Rev 2011;40:961-1005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes and Nafion nanocomposite membranes for use in fuel cell applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tohidian M, Ghaffarian SR. Surface modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes and Nafion nanocomposite membranes for use in fuel cell applications. Polym Adv Technol 2018;29:1219-1226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and evaluation of sulfonated-fluorinated poly (arylene ether)s membranes for a proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee HC, Hong HS, Kim YM, Choi SH, Hong MZ, Lee HS, Ki. Preparation and evaluation of sulfonated-fluorinated poly (arylene ether)s membranes for a proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC). Electrochim Acta 2004;49:2315.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dramatic improvement in water retention and proton conductivity in electrically aligned functionalized CNT/SPEEK nanohybrid PEM

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Swati G, Vaibhav K. Dramatic improvement in water retention and proton conductivity in electrically aligned functionalized CNT/SPEEK nanohybrid PEM. Acs Appl Mater Interfaces 2015;7:264-272.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel composite proton-exchange membrane based on proton-conductive glass powders and sulfonated poly (ether ether ketone)
